content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 2 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 204-205 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_002_page_001.jpg, 2TH_002_page_002.jpg

---

**2:1** Now we-ask you, brothers, concerning the coming of-our Lord Jesus Christ and our gathering-together unto him,

**2:2** unto the not quickly being-shaken you from the mind, nor to-be-alarmed, neither through spirit, nor through word, nor through letter as [if] through us, as-that the day of-the-Lord has-stood-upon [us].

**2:3** Let no-one deceive you in-any way, because [it will not come] unless the falling-away comes first, and the man of-lawlessness is-revealed, the son of-destruction,

**2:4** the-[one]-opposing and exalting-himself above every [thing] being-called God or object-of-worship, so-that he into the temple of-God sits-down, showing-forth himself that he is God.

**2:5** Do-you-not-remember that still being with you I-was-saying these-things to-you?

**2:6** And now the-[thing]-restraining you-know, unto the being-revealed him in-his-own time.

**2:7** For the mystery of-lawlessness already is-working; only the-[one]-restraining now [will restrain] until out-of [the] midst he-comes.

**2:8** And then the lawless-[one] will-be-revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will-slay with-the spirit of-his mouth and will-bring-to-nothing by-the manifestation of-his coming,

**2:9** whose coming is according-to [the] working of-Satan in all power and signs and wonders of-falsehood,

**2:10** and in every deceit of-unrighteousness in the-[ones]-perishing, in-return-for-which the love of-the truth they-did-not-receive unto the being-saved them.

**2:11** And because-of this, God sends to-them a-working of-delusion, unto the believing them the falsehood,

**2:12** so-that all might-be-judged, the-[ones] not having-believed the truth but having-delighted in unrighteousness.

**2:13** But we are-obligated to-give-thanks to-God always concerning you, brothers having-been-loved by [the] Lord, because God chose you from [the] beginning unto salvation in sanctification of-[the]-Spirit and faith of-[the]-truth,

**2:14** unto which also he-called you through our good-news, unto [the] obtaining of-[the]-glory of-our Lord Jesus Christ.

**2:15** So then, brothers, stand-firm and hold-fast the traditions which you-were-taught, whether through word whether through letter of-us.

**2:16** Now our Lord Jesus Christ himself and God the Father of-us, the-[one]-having-loved us and having-given [us] eternal encouragement and good hope in grace,

**2:17** encourage your hearts and establish [you] in every good work and word.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **2:2 — "day of the Lord"**: Sinaiticus reads ΗΜΕΡΑ ΤΟΥ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ (hemera tou kyriou), using the nomen sacrum ΚΥ for ΚΥΡΙΟΥ. This is the reading "day of the Lord" rather than "day of Christ" (ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ) found in some later manuscripts. The Sinaiticus reading aligns with the OT prophetic formula.

2. **2:2 — "has stood upon" (enesteken)**: The Greek ΕΝΕΣΤΗΚΕΝ is the perfect active indicative of ΕΝΙΣΤΗΜΙ, meaning "has come" or "has arrived" or more literally "has stood upon/is present." The Thessalonians were alarmed by the claim that the Day of the Lord had already arrived.

3. **2:3 — "man of lawlessness" (ho anthropos tes anomias)**: Sinaiticus reads ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟC ΤΗC ΑΝΟΜΙΑC — "the man of lawlessness." Some manuscripts (including D, the Majority Text) read ΑΜΑΡΤΙΑC ("sin") instead of ΑΝΟΜΙΑC ("lawlessness"). The Sinaiticus reading ΑΝΟΜΙΑC is followed here, supported also by B and other early witnesses.

4. **2:4 — "showing forth himself that he is God"**: Sinaiticus reads ΑΠΟΔΕΙΚΝΥΝΤΑ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΟΤΙ ΕCΤΙΝ ΘΕΟC. The participle ΑΠΟΔΕΙΚΝΥΝΤΑ ("showing forth, demonstrating, proclaiming") conveys the pretension of the man of lawlessness to divine status. Note: Sinaiticus omits ΩC ("as") before ΘΕΟΝ, reading simply "that he is God" rather than "that he is as God." This is a significant variant.

5. **2:7 — "until out of the midst he comes"**: The Greek ΕΩC ΕΚ ΜΕCΟΥ ΓΕΝΗΤΑΙ is notoriously difficult. Rendered literally: "until out of [the] midst he-comes/becomes." The identity of "the one restraining" (ὁ κατέχων) remains one of the most debated questions in Pauline interpretation.

6. **2:8 — "will slay" (anelei)**: Sinaiticus reads ΑΝΕΛΕΙ (anelei, "will slay/will destroy"), from ΑΝΑΙΡΕΩ. Some manuscripts read ΑΝΑΛΩΣΕΙ (analōsei, "will consume"), from ΑΝΑΛΙΣΚΩ. The Sinaiticus reading "will slay" is the stronger term.

7. **2:8 — "Lord Jesus"**: Sinaiticus reads Ο ΚC ΙC (ho kyrios Iesous), expanding the nomina sacra. Some manuscripts add ΧΡΙΣΤΟC (Christos) after ΙΗΣΟΥC. Sinaiticus has the shorter reading.

8. **2:11 — "God sends" (pempei)**: Sinaiticus reads ΠΕΜΠΕΙ — present tense, "sends," not future "will send" (ΠΕΜΨΕΙ) as in some manuscripts. The present tense may convey divine action already set in motion or a timeless theological principle.

9. **2:12 — "might be judged" (krithōsin)**: Sinaiticus reads ΚΡΙΘΩCΙΝ — "might be judged." Some manuscripts read ΚΑΤΑΚΡΙΘΩCΙΝ ("might be condemned"), a stronger compound verb. The Sinaiticus reading uses the simpler ΚΡΙΝΩ.

10. **2:13 — "from the beginning" (ap' archēs)**: Sinaiticus reads ΑΠ ΑΡΧΗC — "from [the] beginning." An important variant exists: some manuscripts (including B) read ΑΠΑΡΧΗΝ (aparchēn, "as firstfruits"). The difference is a single letter in uncial script. The Sinaiticus reading "from the beginning" emphasizes God's eternal election; the variant "as firstfruits" would emphasize the Thessalonians' priority in salvation.

11. **2:15 — "traditions" (paradoseis)**: ΠΑΡΑΔΟCΕΙC — rendered literally as "traditions," meaning "things handed over/transmitted." Paul uses the same word positively here for apostolic teaching that he uses negatively elsewhere for human traditions opposing God's word. The context determines the sense.

12. **2:16 — Word order "Jesus Christ himself and God the Father"**: Sinaiticus places ΑΥΤΟC ΔΕ Ο ΚC ΗΜΩΝ ΙC ΧC ΚΑΙ Ο ΘC Ο ΠΑΤΗΡ ΗΜΩΝ — "our Lord Jesus Christ himself and God the Father of us." The unusual word order, placing Christ before God the Father, may reflect the christological emphasis of the preceding context. The singular verb in v. 17 (ΠΑΡΑΚΑΛΕCΑΙ, "may he encourage") grammatically treats Father and Son as a unity.

13. **2:16 — "eternal encouragement"**: Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΡΑΚΛΗCΙΝ ΑΙΩΝΙΑΝ — "eternal encouragement/comfort." The same root ΠΑΡΑΚΑΛΕΩ appears in the verb of v. 17 ("encourage"), creating a wordplay: the God who gives eternal encouragement is asked to encourage their hearts.

14. **2:17 — "work and word"**: Sinaiticus reads ΕΡΓΩ ΚΑΙ ΛΟΓΩ — "work and word." Some manuscripts reverse the order to "word and work" (ΛΟΓΩ ΚΑΙ ΕΡΓΩ). The Sinaiticus order places practice before speech.

15. **General note — "the man of lawlessness" passage (2:3-12)**: This passage is among the most debated in the Pauline corpus. The Sinaiticus text presents it without paragraph breaks or punctuation. Key interpretive terms — "the restrainer" (τὸ κατέχον / ὁ κατέχων), "the temple of God," "the mystery of lawlessness" — are translated with absolute literalness, preserving the ambiguity of the original. No interpretive glosses are imposed.

— EMET
